attributes.stem: A 47-year-old woman presents to her General Practice clinic with a six-month history of itchy, scaly skin on her left lower leg. She reports a long-standing history of varicose veins and swelling in both legs, worse on the left. Her medical history includes obesity and hypertension. She does not take any regular medications. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. The photograph shows the changes on her leg (Image). There is pitting oedema to the mid-calf bilaterally.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: High ligation and stripping of the great saphenous vein
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with signs and symptoms consistent with chronic venous insufficiency and varicose eczema. The photograph shows changes of hyperpigmentation, erythema and scaling on the skin on the medial aspect of her leg. The most appropriate first-line management for this condition is compression stockings. Compression therapy is the cornerstone of management for chronic venous insufficiency and its associated complications, including varicose eczema. It helps to reduce oedema, improve venous return, and alleviate symptoms such as itching and discomfort.

High ligation and stripping of GSV (great saphenous vein) is a surgical procedure used to treat varicose veins. While it may be considered as a treatment option in some cases, it is not the most appropriate first-line management for this patient. Endovenous laser ablation has also taken over as the recommended option ahead of high ligation and venous stripping.

Endovenous laser ablation is a minimally invasive procedure used to treat varicose veins. While it may be considered as a treatment option in the future, it is not the most appropriate first-line management for this patient. Conservative measures, including compression therapy, should be tried first.

Topical corticosteroids can be useful for short-term management of acute flares of varicose eczema. However, they are not the most appropriate first-line treatment for the underlying chronic venous insufficiency. They may be used as an adjunct to compression therapy if the eczema is particularly symptomatic.

Sclerotherapy is a procedure used to treat small to medium-sized varicose veins. While it may be considered as part of a comprehensive treatment plan, it is not the most appropriate first-line management for this patient's condition.

In addition to compression therapy, this patient should be advised on lifestyle modifications such as weight loss, regular exercise, and leg elevation when resting. These measures, combined with compression therapy, form the basis of conservative management for chronic venous insufficiency and varicose eczema.
learningPoints: Compression therapy is the cornerstone of management for chronic venous insufficiency and varicose eczema.
Lifestyle modifications, including weight loss and regular exercise, are important adjuncts in managing chronic venous insufficiency.
Topical corticosteroids can be used for short-term management of varicose eczema flares.
references: A synopsis of current international guidelines and new modalities for the treatment of varicose veins. https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2017/april/a-synopsis-of-current-international-guidelines-2
